message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Clinical biomarkers that accurately predict mortality are needed for the effective management of patients with severe COVID-19 illness.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine whether D-dimer levels after anticoagulation treatment is predictive of in-hospital mortality.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Retrospective study using electronic health record data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A large New York City hospital network serving a diverse, urban patient population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Adult patients hospitalized for severe COVID-19 infection who received therapeutic anticoagulation for thromboprophylaxis between February 25, 2020 and May 31, 2020.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Mean and trend of D-dimer levels in the 3 days following the first therapeutic dose of anticoagulation.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcomes</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>In-hospital mortality versus discharge.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>1835 adult patients (median age, 67 years [interquartile range, 57-78]; 58% male) with PCR-confirmed COVID-19 who received therapeutic anticoagulation during hospitalization were included. 74% (1365) of patients were discharged and 26% (430) died in hospital. The study cohort was divided into four groups based on the mean D-dimer levels and its trend following anticoagulation initiation, with significantly different in-hospital mortality rates (p<0.001): 49% for the high mean-increase trend (HI) group; 27% for the high-decrease (HD) group; 21% for the low-increase (LI) group; and 9% for the low-decrease (LD) group. Using penalized logistic regression models to simultaneously analyze 67 variables (baseline demographics, comorbidities, vital signs, laboratory values, D-dimer levels), post-anticoagulant D-dimer groups had the highest adjusted odds ratios (ORadj) for predicting in-hospital mortality. The ORadj of in-hospital death among patients from the HI group was 6.58 folds (95% CI 3.81-11.16) higher compared to the LD group. The LI (ORadj: 4.06, 95% CI 2.23-7.38) and HD (ORadj: 2.37; 95% CI 1.37-4.09) groups were also associated with higher mortality compared to the LD group.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions and Relevance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>D-dimer levels and its trend following the initiation of anticoagulation have high and independent predictive value for in-hospital mortality. This novel prognostic biomarker should be incorporated into management protocols to guide resource allocation and prospective studies for emerging treatments in hospitalized COVID-19 patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key Points</jats:title>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Are D-dimer levels following therapeutic anticoagulation predictive of mortality in hospitalized COVID-19 patients?</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Finding</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>In a retrospective study of 1835 adult COVID-19 patients who received therapeutic anticoagulation for thromboprophylaxis during hospitalization, 1365 (74%) patients were discharged and 470 (26%) died. Post-anticoagulant D-dimer levels and trends were significantly and independently predictive of mortality.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                  <jats:sec>
                    <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                    <jats:p>Active monitoring of post-anticoagulant D-dimer levels in hospitalized COVID-19 patients is a novel strategy for stratifying individual risk of in-hospital mortality that can help guide resource allocation and prospective studies for emerging treatments for severe COVID-19 illness.</jats:p>
                  </jats:sec>
                </jats:sec>
title: Post-Anticoagulant D-dimer as a Highly Prognostic Biomarker of COVID-19 Mortality

reference.unstructured: World Health Organization. Coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) Situation Report – 209. Published online August 16, 2020.
Buuren S van , Groothuis-Oudshoorn K . mice: Multivariate imputation by chained equations in R. J Stat Softw. Published online 2010:1--68.
Hastie T , Tibshirani R , Wainwright M . Statistical Learning with Sparsity: The Lasso and Generalizations. CRC press; 2015.

reference.article-title: The prediction role of D-dimer in recurrence of venous thromboembolism 1-year after anticoagulation discontinuing following idiopathic deep vein thrombosis
reference.journal-title: J Res Med Sci Off J Isfahan Univ Med Sci
